text: India ink is visible near the tumor site as the surgeon begins medial retroperitoneal dissection of the mesocolon. An electric scalpel is inserted through the right lower five millimeter port, while three point five millimeter forceps are used through the other ports. The dissection progresses cranially.

The surgeon exposes and cuts the root of the inferior mesenteric artery using an ultrasonic device after clipping. Retroperitoneal dissection proceeds laterally, identifying and preserving the left ureter and gonadal vessels. The inferior mesenteric vein and left colic artery are clipped and cut, and the lateral attachment is separated by dissecting the taut fusion fascia, continuing to the median dissection plane.

The surgeon dissects the retrolectal space, carefully identifying and preserving the bilateral hypogastric nerves. The retroperitoneum on both the right and left sides of the upper rectum is dissected. The mesocolon is further dissected, exposing the colonic walls along the cut line of the distal cone.

A five-millimeter scope is inserted through the right lower port, while forceps, an intestinal clip, and an articulated linear stapler are inserted through the twelve-millimeter port for colonic transection. An intestinal clip is placed at the oral side of the colonic cut line, with adjustable angles using an angle adjuster. The specimen is extracted through a small skin incision at the umbilicus, and an anastomosis is performed using a double stapling technique.
A nine-inch French drain is inserted into the pelvis through the right lower five-millimeter incision. The small incision wounds are barely visible twenty-five days postoperatively.
